title: CMC: CANNABIS MEDIA COUNCIL — I'M HIGH RIGHT NOW
summary: Shifted cannabis stigma by featuring Baby Boomers as confident, stylish users

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text:
We built the Cannabis Media Council's first consumer campaign around a single provocation: "I'm High Right Now." The campaign featured Baby Boomers photographed in bold, fashion-forward portraits — active, sharp, stylish, undeniably present. It addressed a critical gap: the generation most affected by anti-cannabis propaganda had become one of the fastest-growing segments of legal cannabis users, yet appeared in no mainstream cannabis advertising.

By flipping the most common misconception about cannabis — that it makes people visibly impaired — into a declaration of agency, the campaign normalized cannabis more effectively than typical messaging aimed at younger audiences. It also met a strategic second objective: showing publishers that cannabis ads could be sophisticated and brand-safe. Once one publisher accepted a cannabis ad, others followed.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: The Cannabis Media Council is a U.S. trade group created to help normalize cannabis use and open up mainstream advertising for the legal cannabis industry. Even as legalization spread across most American states, advertising options lagged behind. Decades of War on Drugs messaging shaped media policy. Cannabis brands could not buy the same ad placements that alcohol or pharmaceutical brands used routinely.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Educate and reduce stigma among Americans, especially Baby Boomers, while demonstrating to mainstream publishers that cannabis ads are high-quality and culturally relevant.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The War on Drugs shaped how older Americans think about cannabis. Most marketing targeted younger audiences already on board, reinforcing the stigma it claimed to be breaking. Publishers saw cannabis ads as risky because there were no high-quality examples in mainstream media.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if, instead of making cannabis seem cool to young people, the campaign showed that the most unexpected cannabis users were already using it and thriving?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Baby Boomers are the most overlooked group in cannabis advertising and the most affected by past propaganda, yet they represent one of the fastest-growing groups of legal cannabis users. A campaign featuring real Boomers could normalize cannabis more than content aimed at 25-year-olds — while proving to publishers that cannabis ads can be sophisticated.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We wanted to hyper-normalize cannabis acceptance by showing how radically normal being stoned is. We co-opted the most common misconception around cannabis use and flipped it into a declaration of agency. Boomers photographed in bold, fashion-forward portraits conveyed: these people are high right now, and they are doing just fine. We designed visuals and messaging to feel like a fashion editorial, meeting the brand-safety standards of hesitant publishers.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Print advertising (including Vanity Fair), programmatic digital display across all 50 states, digital advertorial and native placements, streaming audio, and organic social. Media strategy targeted legal markets with paid buys while using earned media to reach national audiences.

proof: 286M earned media impressions (87:1 earned-to-paid ratio)
18.3M paid impressions reaching all 50 states
4 Clio Awards plus 2 shortlists
First cannabis advertisement in Vanity Fair print edition
~2x industry average dwell time on mobile (1:56)
287% LinkedIn follower growth; 190% Instagram growth for CMC
Unlocked new cannabis ad space with Hearst, TrafficJunky, and Conde Nast
team: Advertiser / Brand: Cannabis Media Council, New York
Chief Creative Officer: Amanda Wood / Sister Merci
Chief Strategy Officer: Paul Lawton / Sister Merci

title: CASINOTIME: BIG FRAN
summary: Created female Sasquatch mascot that forced market correction in Ontario iGaming

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Ontario's regulated iGaming market defaulted to sign-up bonuses and celebrity endorsements. We identified a gap: the fastest-growing segment of online gambling was women over 50, yet no brand addressed them. We created Big Fran, a Pinot Grigio-loving, bingo-playing female Sasquatch who served as a mirror of her audience. Through deadpan wit and an inviting presence, Fran tapped into something more powerful than adrenaline: community and belonging.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Ontario opened its regulated iGaming market in April 2022, leading to a surge of new online casino brands competing for the same audience with nearly identical products. CasinoTime needed to stand out against industry giants like Bet365 and FanDuel. The category defaulted to two approaches: sign-up bonuses and celebrity endorsements. Most brands used flashy, high-energy ads.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Develop CasinoTime from naming through launch and build brand preference in a market where the product is functionally identical across platforms and competitors outspent us by an order of magnitude.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: iGaming in Ontario is regulated with strict responsible gambling disclosures. Most platforms run the same games from the same providers. Category leaders spent ten times CasinoTime's media budget. The brand needed to win on identity, not impressions.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: Instead of selling the fantasy of fast money, could an online casino brand focus on what people are actually seeking: connection?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Research from Rutgers University and the National Library of Medicine showed women over 50 were one of the fastest-growing segments in online gambling — yet women are almost completely absent from iGaming advertising outside of playing the role of eye candy. When we spoke with women over 50 who play bingo, we learned they were looking for community, not adrenaline.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We created Big Fran as the brand's anchor. While the category used overproduced casino glitz, Fran welcomed players into her forest cave for wine and chat. TV spots were treated as an invitation to an overlooked audience. The visual system — black and gold, playful typography, smiley-face logomark — was designed to feel more like entertainment than gambling.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: The campaign ran across national TV, OOH, paid social, digital display, in-app creative, and branded merchandise. We extended the work into in-product UX, developing design elements that reinforced community positioning inside the gaming experience. We adapted the campaign for each of CasinoTime's 10 affiliated charitable gaming centres with localized variations.

proof: 800M+ targeted paid impressions in one market
2% brand awareness increase in 6 months on 10x less media spend than category leaders
Industry shift: competitors moved to softer, inclusive messaging within months
One fan tattooed the brand logo
Two TV spots plus full asset library produced in a single 24-hour shoot
Campaign adapted across 10 affiliated charitable gaming centres

title: J.R. STRAIN: ROOTSUIT
summary: Used AI to build a visual icon that introduced aeroponics to Ontario cannabis consumers

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: J.R. Strain grew cannabis using aeroponics — a method where plants grow without soil. Almost no one knew what aeroponics was. We used AI (Runway ML, MidJourney) to connect the abstract product truth to a visual: a life-sized, human-shaped costume made of white roots. The Rootsuit became the visual anchor of the brand identity across campaign photography, OOH, dispensary displays, and digital creative.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: J.R. Strain grew cannabis using aeroponics. But almost no one knew what aeroponics was, no one was asking for it, and the brand had no presence in Ontario. J.R. Strain had a real product difference, but consumers could not see or understand it.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Make cannabis consumers aware of aeroponic cannabis and position J.R. Strain as Ontario's top aeroponic brand.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Cannabis consumers care most about THC and are wary of brands. The legal market is crowded with new brands monthly. Technical explanations of aeroponics would not work because people do not read detailed copy in dispensaries. Cannabis Act rules against mascots limited visual assets.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if consumers were more likely to engage with mystery than explanation? Rather than functional storytelling, could we spark curiosity?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Aeroponics creates a unique root structure: dense, white, and exposed. A full-body root sculpture would stand out on a shelf and prompt curiosity where a functional explanation would not.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We used AI to connect aeroponic root structures to a human-sized form that adhered to Cannabis Act mascot regulations. AI outputs guided costume designers in building the final suit. Campaign language reinforced the roots positioning.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: With Runway ML and Midjourney, we explored how aeroponic root structures could take human-sized form. AI outputs guided costume designers in building the final suit. The campaign language reinforced roots positioning with lines like: "We're your guy with the roots to prove it." "You will not find dirt on us because we do not use any." The integrated launch ran across brand voice development, a visual identity rebrand, refreshed sales collateral, PR, print, digital display, audio placements, dispensary OOH, and event activations.

proof: Complete sell-out on OCS within one week of launch
51% surge in average weekly sales in Toronto; 38% additional boost post-KIND event
39% sales increase across Ontario
2,000 additional retail purchase orders
3M+ targeted Ontario impressions in three weeks
Coverage in Campaign Canada
team: Paul Lawton: Qualitative and quantitative research, campaign strategy, strategic unlock, brand architecture
Amanda Wood: Creative Director, Design, Copy, Costume Development

title: COLLECTIVE ARTS: DRINK CREATIVELY
summary: Reclaimed creative leadership by making scale of artist network visible

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: We framed this through Jenni Romaniuk's Distinctive Brand Assets framework: Collective Arts had a distinctiveness crisis, not a differentiation crisis. Competitors had copied the surface without replicating the underlying asset. We produced "Drink Creatively," a brand video where every shot featured different artist-submitted work brought to life through VFX, with the product at centre.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Collective Arts Brewing was among Canada's original craft breweries to feature world-class art on packaging. This visual identity became recognizable and iconic. But after years of success, competitors copied the approach, making art-forward packaging less distinctive. Collective Arts had never run paid advertising.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Launch Collective Arts' first-ever paid campaign to reassert creative leadership and remind consumers that Collective Arts is as creative on the inside as on the outside.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The brand's main differentiator, art on packaging, had been diluted by imitators. Collective Arts needed to reclaim creative leadership without repeating what competitors were now doing. They were starting from scratch with paid media: no past data, no benchmarks.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the campaign didn't need to defend creative credentials but made the scale and depth of the artist network visible for the first time?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: We framed this through Distinctive Brand Assets framework. Collective Arts had a distinctiveness crisis, not a differentiation crisis. Competitors had copied the surface-level expression without replicating the underlying asset. The campaign needed to re-establish uniqueness on the Distinctive Asset Grid by showing what no imitator could replicate.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We developed "Drink Creatively" as the campaign platform. We produced a brand video that functioned as a Distinctive Brand Asset activation. Every shot featured different artist-submitted work brought to life through VFX and motion design, with the product at centre. The creative approach followed Romaniuk's formation formula: reach, co-presentation, and consistency.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: The campaign ran across paid social (media hero), digital display, a branded landing page, YouTube, and advertorial content partnership with Toronto Life. Media strategy treated paid social as the primary driver of reach, with programmatic and display as supporting channels.

proof: 17.3M impressions against 16.5M goal (105% of target)
Paid social generated 10.5M impressions, 11.5x goal
Brand video became top-performing organic video on YouTube channel
Measurable increase in organic content performance across platforms
team: Steve St. Jean, Creative Director, Collective Arts
Toni Shelton, Brand Director, Collective Arts

title: UP CANNABIS: MEA CULPA
summary: Turned negative reviews into campaign creative, rebuilding trust and lifting sales 130%

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: UP Cannabis had a trust problem after legalization-era quality issues. We built the campaign around real negative reviews, quoting them exactly and crediting original usernames. Each ad spoke directly to a specific critic, showing their complaint and responding with an apology and the UP20 guarantee. By acknowledging frustrations instead of ignoring them, we turned critics into supporters.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: UP Cannabis struggled after legalization. The rush to market on October 17, 2018, led to issues: dry flower, weak potency, inconsistent quality. UP responded by improving growing methods and launching a new lineup with a 20% or higher THC guarantee. Still, the brand faced a trust problem.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Encourage Canadian cannabis consumers to reconsider UP Cannabis by acknowledging past failures and reintroducing the product with a credible quality guarantee.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Regaining trust in cannabis is difficult. When customers get a bad product, they talk about it publicly. UP's negative reviews were out in the open. Strict Cannabis Act rules meant we could not use product sampling to win people back.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the best way for a cannabis brand to regain trust after letting people down was to do something very Canadian: apologize, not generic, but using the negative reviews themselves as creative?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: We would use the negative reviews themselves as our creative, using real customer words to show we were listening.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built the campaign around real negative reviews, quoting them exactly and crediting original usernames. Each ad spoke directly to a specific critic, showing their complaint and responding with an apology and the UP20 guarantee. The tone was self-aware, friendly, and very Canadian.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We refreshed the brand identity and ran creative across print, out-of-home, digital, paid social, and advertorials, as well as a new website. The campaign focused on Reddit and cannabis Twitter, where complaints first appeared.

proof: 130% lift in sales (OCS data + brand lift study)
24.3M impressions, 117% of target, at $9.64 CPM
4.7M earned social impressions from cannabis subreddits + 140K+ on Twitter
Strategy Magazine: top three most-read brand articles of the year
team: Paul Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer: Brand Architecture, Brand Identity Refresh, Go-to-Market Plan, Media Strategy and Buying
Amanda Wood, Chief Creative Officer: Development, Brand Identity Refresh, Print, OOH, Digital

title: SCOTIABANK: HOCKEY 24
summary: Ethnographic research revealed hockey's deepest meaning is community, not competition

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Scotiabank was tied for first in unaided recall as a hockey sponsor but struggled to demonstrate fit between master brand and sponsorship properties. Over two months of ethnographic fieldwork in community rinks, we found that parents do not put their kids in hockey for exercise or fun. Seventy-five percent of the values parents cited were social. Hockey's deepest meaning is community formation, not competition. This insight became the foundation for Hockey 24: A Film by Canada, a 90-minute feature-length documentary.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Scotiabank held sponsorship positions with the NHL, Hockey Night in Canada, and individual teams. The bank was tied for first in unaided recall as a hockey sponsor, but had a specific weakness: it fell short in demonstrating fit between master brand and sponsorship properties. The existing "Heroes of Hockey Day" campaign produced adequate branded content but did nothing to deepen emotional connection.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Redefine Scotiabank's hockey content strategy by grounding it in real insight about what hockey means to Canadian families, and use that research to shift the campaign toward content people actually want to watch.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Sponsorship marketing in hockey defaults to logo placement and manufactured enthusiasm. Scotiabank's existing content sat firmly in the second category. The brand's own research showed that awareness of sponsorship properties influenced performance, but the content itself was not building affinity that justified the spend.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the research revealed something that contradicted category assumptions, something that could reshape how the company talked about hockey entirely?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Over two months of ethnographic fieldwork, interviewing parents, coaches, referees, and players, we found that parents do not put kids in hockey for exercise or fun. Seventy-five percent of values parents cited were social. Twenty-five percent were individual. Hockey's deepest meaning is community formation, not competition.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We used ethnographic findings to build a tension map that reframed the campaign. The core tensions: childhood is being eroded by technology; today's youth lack grit, but hockey builds it; it is harder to form lasting friendships, but hockey is intensely social. This framework pivoted "Heroes of Hockey Day" away from manufactured content toward real community stories. It became the foundation for Hockey 24: a 90-minute feature-length documentary inviting all Canadians to submit video footage capturing a single day in community hockey. Twenty-five professional documentary crews were dispatched across the country.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Ethnographic research conducted over two months in community rinks across the GTA, working alongside cultural anthropologist Stephanie Creighton. We interviewed 14+ informants across parents, coaches, referees, community centre staff, and players. The research surfaced core values and tensions. The framework became the foundation for Hockey 24, a participatory film project that centred stories from LGBTQ community, new Canadians, Indigenous communities, and people with physical disabilities.

proof: 500,000+ Canadian homes for premiere; 3rd most-watched show that day
99% lift in brand awareness with improvements in propensity and recommendation
CMA Gold (Business Impact), CMA Silver (Innovative Media)
Clio Sports Bronze, The One Show Finalist
Official Selection at 12+ international film festivals
team: PK Lawton: Lead Strategist, Ethnographic Research, Audience Analysis, Strategic Narrative
Stephanie Creighton: Cultural Anthropologist, Ethnographic Research
Agency: The Mark (Hayes Steinberg, CCO/EP; Kyle Scotland and Jason Sweeney, Directors)
Client: Scotiabank (Clinton Braganza, CMO; Mike Tasevski, Sponsorship Marketing)

title: TWEED: HI / DON'T DRIVE HIGH
summary: Built Canada's most recognized cannabis brand through education and responsibility

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: On October 17, 2018, Canada legalized recreational cannabis. We developed "Hi." — a simple phrase that introduced Tweed and gave a playful nod to cannabis culture. The campaign sent people to Tweed.com for answers to real questions. After legalization, most marketing was banned. We built "Don't Drive High," a partnership with MADD Canada and Uber, using the public safety exemption to maintain brand presence through channels that would otherwise be restricted.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: On October 17, 2018, Canada became the first G7 country to legalize recreational cannabis. Most people did not know the difference between THC and CBD and held many misconceptions. The client wanted people to ask for Tweed, not just weed. Canopy Growth put their existing medical brand, Tweed, at the centre.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Build brand awareness during pre-legalization window and establish Tweed as the first cannabis brand Canadians would think of when legalization arrived. Then, keep Tweed visible after legalization even though most traditional cannabis marketing was now banned.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: "Hi.": The time frame was short and results would last. Any brand awareness built before October 17 would endure, since new marketing would not be allowed after. The target audience was beyond the core cannabis consumer to include regular Canadians who were anxious, uninformed, and skeptical. "Don't Drive High": After October 17, Tweed could not use out-of-home ads, sponsor events, use lifestyle advertising, or broadcast. All usual marketing channels were closed.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: "Hi.": Information, not aspiration, was what Canadians needed. The brand that showed up as a friendly, approachable educator would own the space that actually mattered: trust. "Don't Drive High": Could a genuine public safety campaign become the marketing vehicle for a cannabis brand post-legalization?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: "Hi.": Information, not aspiration. "Don't Drive High": The Cannabis Act did not allow cannabis brands to use out-of-home advertising, but public safety campaigns could. By making the campaign a true social-impact effort focused on preventing impaired driving, Tweed could use restricted channels.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: "Hi.": Developed the strategy around "Hi.", a simple phrase that introduced the brand. The campaign sent people to Tweed.com where Canadians could find answers. Education was the core. "Don't Drive High": Built a three-way partnership with MADD Canada and Uber Canada. Tweed brought category credibility; the world's largest cannabis company proactively telling consumers not to drive high was unprecedented. The creative suggested 101 things Canadians could do instead of driving (baking a cookie, popping bubble wrap, calling grandparents). The tone was playful, using humour to share a serious message.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: "Hi.": Campaign ran across out-of-home ads nationwide, restaurant and bar activations on 4/20, digital and mobile ads, Live Nation concert partnerships, over 250 events across the country including Pride and Calgary Stampede, PR through Citizen Relations, and content through Merry Jane. Tweed also launched the Tweed Collective, a $20 million, four-year commitment to social initiatives. "Don't Drive High": Campaign used out-of-home ads (first for cannabis brand under Cannabis Act), pre-roll video, radio, and interactive website at DontDriveHigh.ca.

proof: 700M+ media impressions | 190M paid impressions
38% lift in brand awareness | 95% month-over-month website traffic increase
34,000 consumers signed up for Tweed communications
"Don't Drive High": AToMiC Gold x3, SIA Silver, FIA Best Road Safety Intervention
First cannabis brand to deploy OOH under Cannabis Act
40,000 Uber promo codes distributed
team: "Hi.", Agency: Cossette (Wes Wolsh, CSO; Sarah Thompson / PK Lawton, VP Strategy; Natasha Michalowska and Cooper Evoy, ACD). Cossette Media, Citizen Relations (PR), Behaviour (Experiential), Merry Jane (Content). Client: Canopy Growth (David Bigioni, CMO).

"Don't Drive High", Agency: Cossette (Peter Ignazi and Carlos Moreno, Global CCOs; Paul Lawton, VP Strategy; Rosie Gentile, SVP Strategy). Cossette Media, Citizen Relations. Client: Canopy Growth (David Bigioni, CCO), MADD Canada (Andrew Murie, CEO), Uber Canada (Lindsay Liptok, Head of Marketing).

title: COLEMAN: GET OUTSIDE DAY
summary: Tripled year-over-year media coverage by turning a brand into a civic movement

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Coleman Canada was well known for products but had no cultural presence outside camping season. We created Get Outside Day as a Coleman-owned platform built around a petition to the Canadian government, giving every channel and touchpoint a single, clear call-to-action. The petition mechanic unified earned, social, retail, and digital under one organizing principle — solving the problem of integrated campaigns where every channel runs disconnected KPIs.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Coleman Canada wanted to stay relevant in a mature outdoor recreation market where people thought about the brand once a year at the start of camping season. The company was well known for products but lacked cultural presence.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create a platform that kept Coleman in the conversation year-round, connected the brand to a bigger cultural idea, and generated earned media far beyond what the budget would suggest.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Outdoor gear is seasonal and practical. People do not follow a tent company on social media for entertainment. The category's social presence was quiet. Coleman's Facebook page had fewer than 4,000 followers.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if we gave people a reason to engage with the brand that was not about buying a cooler, but about supporting a civic effort?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: A petition works as a campaign mechanic because it gives every audience touchpoint a single, clear action. Media relations, social content, retail partnerships, and the microsite all point at the same call-to-action. That unified action solves the chronic problem of integrated campaigns.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We created Get Outside Day as a Coleman-owned platform and launched it in 2014. The movement frame positioned Coleman not as a brand selling tents but as the company leading a national effort. The petition to declare a national holiday gave the campaign a civic dimension, earning media coverage far beyond what a product campaign could generate.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We built GetOutsideCanada.com as the campaign hub and filled it with broad, accessible content. The Walmart "30 Ideas" page averaged over two minutes per visit. We brought in Dr. Dina Kulik, a pediatrician with media credibility, to lead broadcast segments. We designed the action spectrum framework, first developed for Sunbeam, adding co-produce and produce tiers to capture the petition mechanic and UGC volume. We mapped every channel and content type to a specific engagement tier.

proof: 2014: 19M impressions, 68 media hits, Facebook engagement increased 12x
2015: 59M impressions (3x the 20M target), 228 media stories, 10.6% engagement rate (vs. 2.5% average)
70.4% petition conversion rate, 2,500+ pieces of UGC
Coleman declared July 15th Get Outside Day
team: PK Lawton, Strategy and Media Buying
Jennifer Meehan and Heather Meehan, Account Leads
Client: Coleman Canada / Jarden Consumer Solutions (Ezio Sbrizzi, President; Dawn Whiteside, Client Lead)
Partners: Walmart Canada, Canadian Tire (Retail), Dr. Dina Kulik (Spokesperson)

title: SUNBEAM: SUPPORTS WITH WARMTH
summary: Built measurement system that made commodity brand most-engaged in category

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Sunbeam Canada was relaunching its electric blanket line with no brand story and no budget for paid media. We partnered with the Canadian Cancer Society's Wheels of Hope program to connect Sunbeam's core value of warmth to volunteer drivers who keep cancer patients warm on their way to treatment. We created a five-level measurement model (applause, share, simple engagement, deep engagement, action) and adjusted content in real time. Cause-story content consistently outperformed product and giveaway content on both reach and engagement depth.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Sunbeam Canada was bringing back its electric blanket line in a market with no real loyalty and no brand story. Heated blankets are seasonal, low-interest products that often go unnoticed.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Build lasting brand awareness through a campaign that would earn media coverage, spark social engagement, and give people a reason to care about Sunbeam beyond seeing it in stores.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The category was quiet. Canadian competitors such as KitchenAid and Oster were running product promotions but getting almost no engagement. Sunbeam's Facebook page had fewer than 1,200 followers.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the most engaging content was not about the brand itself, but about real people whose stories connected to the brand's core value?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: A first-person story from a Wheels of Hope volunteer driver generated significantly higher engagement than the next-best-performing product post. The brand's role was to step back and let people tell their own stories.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We partnered with Sunbeam and the Canadian Cancer Society's Wheels of Hope program. We connected Sunbeam's core value of warmth to the Wheels of Hope mission, where volunteer drivers help keep cancer patients warm and supported. This was more than putting a logo on a check.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We created a proprietary five-level measurement model: applause (likes), share (word-of-mouth), simple engagement (comments), deep engagement (UGC), and action (volunteering, donating, buying). Each type of content and channel matched a specific level. We adjusted in real time.

proof: 23M earned impressions, 108 placements over three cycles
19.5% peak engagement rate (8x industry average)
Facebook grew 73% during November 2015 push
Only brand in Canadian home comfort generating meaningful social engagement
team: PK Lawton, Strategy and Media Buying
Client: Jarden Canada / Newell Brands (Jamie Libregts, Client Lead)

title: MADEGOOD: FOR ALL THE GOOD REASONS
summary: Shifted snack brand from lunchbox niche to values-led identity across occasions

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: MadeGood had a loyal Canadian following as an allergen-free lunchbox snack. In the U.S., the market was larger and more crowded, which limited reach. We built a brand architecture connecting real product advantages (free from top eight allergens, B-Corp certified) to emotional truth. Research showed the core audience — "The Balanced Snacker" — was not after the cleanest label but a brand that matched their values. We positioned MadeGood around "For All the Good Reasons," with "Highly Thoughtful Snacks" as the consumer-facing idea, shifting from a functional, allergen-free play to a values-led identity.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: MadeGood, owned by Riverside Foods, had a loyal following in Canada as an allergen-free snack for school lunches. In the U.S., the market was bigger and more crowded, and the lunchbox positioning limited reach.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create a master brand architecture, define a consumer persona, and build a content strategy to help MadeGood expand in the U.S. with a clear identity and value.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The U.S. snack market is saturated with better-for-you brands competing on nearly identical claims. MadeGood had real product advantages but no strategic framework to connect features to emotional truth. The brand was pigeonholed as a children's snack, limiting purchase occasions.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the allergen-free story was not about restriction, but about inclusion? What if the brand's real competitive advantage was not any single ingredient claim, but the sheer density of reasons to choose it?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Our research showed that "The Balanced Snacker" was not after the cleanest label or richest treat. They wanted a brand that matched their values but struggled to tell which brands were truly authentic.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We positioned MadeGood around "For All the Good Reasons", a platform that reframed the brand's nutritional depth as proof of genuine care. The strategy shifted the brand from a functional allergen-free play to a values-led identity. We built the full architecture around "The Optimistic Neighbor" personality with "Highly Thoughtful Snacks" as the consumer-facing brand idea.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Research program: qualitative research including in-depth interviews, focus groups across multiple U.S. markets, ethnographic studies, and consumer diaries. Quantitative research including surveys, market segmentation analysis, trend analysis, and consumer behaviour modelling. Tension framework: We built a proprietary tension segmentation model mapping two axes of consumer conflict. Experience vs. Function captured the pull between taste indulgence and health practicality. Idealism vs. Pragmatism mapped the gap between aspirational healthy eating and real-world convenience. This produced four distinct consumer personas. We built a full brand architecture including purpose, promise, unique point, personality, and voice. We created the "Highly Thoughtful Snacks" platform and strategy with a brand manifesto video, influencer partnerships, and full content plan.

proof: 26.9% sales increase in 2023
#43 on Instacart's top 75 innovative, emerging brands
Brand architecture adopted as core operating framework for global marketing team
Strategy informed all subsequent campaigns, product development, and media planning
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy and Research Lead
Jill Geromino, Senior Director of Marketing, MadeGood

title: PANGEA: SIMPLE. HONEST. TRANSFERS.
summary: Turned transactional remittance app into culturally fluent brand for Mexican American diaspora

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Pangea had built a functional remittance platform but the brand had no emotional distinction in a commoditized market. Through listening labs and behavioural research, we found that the audience did not want a brand that understood their culture — they wanted one that respected it enough not to perform understanding. We built the position around "Simple. Honest. Transfers." — not a tagline but a strategic lens applied to every touchpoint. Every communication was evaluated against three questions: Is this simple? Is this honest? Does this get out of the way?

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Pangea had built a functional, reliable remittance platform. Its core users were first- and second-generation Mexican Americans. The brand had no emotional distinction in a market where every competitor led with fees and speed.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Define Pangea's brand meaning for its core audience and build a strategic system that could flex across markets, moments, and media, starting with a Visa co-marketing partnership.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The remittance category is commoditized on price and speed. Most platforms run identical games from same providers. Pangea needed a position that honoured the complexity of sending money without becoming sentimental. It needed to work in English and Spanish, across digital and retail.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the brand's greatest strength was not a feature at all, but the fact that it disappears? The platform works because it gets out of the way of the human act it enables.

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Through listening labs and behavioural research, we found that the audience did not want a brand that understood their culture, they wanted one that respected it enough not to perform understanding.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built the position around "Simple. Honest. Transfers.", not a tagline, but a strategic lens applied to every touchpoint. It moved the brand from fintech jargon to human utility.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Phase 1: audience segmentation combining quantitative data with listening labs and stakeholder workshops. Phase 2: behavioural persona development, we created personas like "Fernando" to ground strategy in lived experience. Phase 3: bilingual messaging framework rooted in behavioural insights, designed to feel native in both languages. Phase 4: strategic communications system structured for organizational adoption. Phase 5: Visa Holiday campaign partnership as real-world stress test, with Meta-first content.

proof: +23% brand-positive sentiment on Meta
Influencer pilots exceeded 2x engagement benchmarks
Visa Holiday campaign: CTR 0.92% / CPV $1.28
Messaging system adopted organization-wide across app store, CRM, and paid media
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Jemilly Castro, Multicultural Strategy and Research
Rachel Boykins, Head of Brand Strategy, Pangea

title: FAR OUT CROPS: BEYOND THE ORDINARY
summary: Built cannabis brand around psychedelic sensory identity that cut through a monotonous shelf

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: The Canadian cannabis shelf is visually monotonous. A licensed producer needed a new brand oriented around the experience of consuming cannabis rather than strain genetics and THC percentages. We built Far Out Crops as a sensory brand first: psychedelic, surreal, unapologetically loud. The positioning framed the brand as one that transcends traditional category norms. The psychedelic visual language became a recognizable brand asset driving organic social engagement.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: A licensed producer needed a new cannabis brand that could stand out in an increasingly commoditized market. The brief was to build something oriented around experience of consuming cannabis.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create a cannabis brand from scratch that resonated with diverse consumer base, educated consumers on experience-oriented cannabis selection, and shifted perceptions in a category stuck in sameness.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The Canadian cannabis shelf is visually monotonous. Standing out requires more than a different colour palette, it requires a completely different visual language.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if a cannabis brand looked and felt like nothing else in the category, from a different world entirely?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Cannabis consumers who prioritize experience over specs are looking for something they cannot easily describe. The brand needed to evoke a feeling before explaining a product.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built Far Out Crops as a sensory brand first and a cannabis brand second. The visual identity was designed to stop people in their tracks, psychedelic, surreal, unapologetically loud.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We developed the brand architecture, establishing positioning, personality, and messaging hierarchy. The visual identity was the centrepiece: bold psychedelic typography (liquid, organic letterforms in purple and black), illustrated artwork with surreal motifs, and a hot pink, orange, and black palette. We designed product packaging, sales and go-to-market collateral, the brand website, and merchandise. The Instagram presence (@faroutcrops) was designed as a visual portfolio.

proof: Far Out Crops launched with a visual identity that was immediately distinctive on shelf and in digital channels
Brand system spanning packaging, merch, social, web was cohesive and unlike any competitor
Psychedelic visual language became recognizable brand asset that drove organic social engagement
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead

title: HIGHLY DUTCH: BEACON OF WEIRD
summary: Reframed organic from certification badge to personality trait and lifestyle signal

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Highly Dutch had strong sales but no coherent identity. Through consumer research and ethnographic work, we identified "High Flyers": 25–45-year-olds for whom authenticity is a core value. The word "organic" resonated not as a product feature but as an ethos. We reframed Highly Dutch as "a beacon of weird" — celebrating the naturally occurring uniqueness in each person. The repositioned brand gave sales teams and budtenders a story they could articulate. The visual rebrand differentiated Highly Dutch on shelf in a category where most organic brands default to earth tones.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Highly Dutch was originally the value-oriented brand from The Green Organic Dutchman. Despite strong sales, the brand had no coherent identity. Sales teams did not know what it stood for.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create a brand identity and messaging strategy that would tell the story of sustainably and ethically grown organic cannabis in a way that resonated with the target audience.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Two problems at once. First, "organic" in cannabis does not carry the same instant credibility as in food. Second, the brand needed to establish independence from parent company while using the one attribute connecting them.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if "organic" was not about what is in the product, but about how you live?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Through consumer research and ethnographic work, we identified "High Flyers": 25-45-year-olds seeking potent, high-quality, good-value dried flower. Authenticity is a core value.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We reframed "organic" from a certification badge to a personality trait. The new Highly Dutch became "a beacon of weird", celebrating the naturally occurring, unmodified uniqueness in each person.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Phase 1: consumer research and ethnography to define the target and map competitive field. Brand repositioning centred on "beacon of weird" concept. New visual identity with bright colours and expressive typography. Channel strategy and media planning. Social media and content strategy. Business analytics to track performance. Phase 2: refined the visual identity with new logo design, tightened brand strategy, and produced asset packages for sales teams.

proof: The repositioned brand gave sales teams and budtenders a story they could articulate
Brand architecture provided internal alignment and external consistency
Visual rebrand differentiated Highly Dutch on shelf in category where most organic brands default to earth tones
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Oli Bell, VP, Marketing and Brand

title: NEIGHBORGOODS: NEIGHBORHOOD CANNABIS CO.
summary: Designed a brand system that feels local everywhere without claiming a specific geography

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: The U.S. cannabis market is fragmented by state. A national-feeling brand risks feeling generic; a hyper-local brand cannot scale. Through focus groups with heavy cannabis consumers, we found that community engagement and local business support were stronger purchase drivers than product attributes. We designed Neighborgoods as a locally adapted brand system: warm retro-diner aesthetics and friendly typography, universal enough to travel but specific enough to feel genuine. Launched with a complete brand system designed for multi-market rollout across U.S. states.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Battle Green Cannabis needed a wholesale brand that could compete across price points in the U.S. cannabis market while maintaining distinct identity.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create a new cannabis brand from scratch, name, identity, architecture, packaging, collateral, that could resonate with diverse cannabis consumers and adapt to local markets without losing brand coherence.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The U.S. cannabis market is fragmented by state, with different regulatory environments, consumer cultures, and competitive conditions. A national-feeling brand risks feeling generic. A hyper-local brand cannot scale.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the brand's identity was the act of being local, not claiming a specific geography, but embodying the values of supporting your neighbourhood?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Through focus groups and interviews with heavy cannabis consumers, we found that community engagement and local business support were stronger purchase drivers than product attributes.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We designed Neighborgoods as a locally adapted brand system. The core identity, visual language, and messaging architecture were built for market-by-market customization while maintaining brand-level consistency.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Audience research and segmentation through focus groups and in-depth interviews with heavy cannabis consumers. We developed the brand name, logo, and visual identity, a retro-Americana aesthetic with teal, cream, and red palette. We designed packaging across product formats. We produced retail and sales collateral, content planning frameworks, and social asset templates. The brand architecture was structured to support local adaptation.

proof: Neighborgoods launched with a complete brand system spanning retail, digital, collateral, and packaging
Designed for multi-market rollout
Brand architecture supports scaling across states while maintaining local identity
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Ben Gaines, Director of Marketing, Wyld Cannabis

title: HOLY MOUNTAIN: A MIRROR, NOT A MEGAPHONE
summary: Built value cannabis brand on ethnographic truth about heavy users, not price

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: The value flower market was growing fast but brands were undifferentiated. Organigram wanted a brand that could earn loyalty from heavy users, not just capture price sensitivity. Through ethnographic research, we found that heavy users carry hostility toward "big weed" — the corporate cannabis companies they see as profiting from a culture those companies did not build. This audience does not want to be educated or elevated. They want to be recognized. We built the brand around "The Hot Couch Guy": an honest, affectionate representation of the heavy user's self-image. Holy Mountain launched in December 2022 with strong consumer reviews.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: The value flower and concentrate market in Canada was growing fast, but brands were largely undifferentiated. Organigram wanted a new brand that could earn loyalty from heavy users.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Develop a new value cannabis brand built from genuine consumer understanding of heavy users, their lifestyle, values, frustrations with existing market.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Heavy cannabis users are the most valuable segment but also the most skeptical. A value brand targeting this audience could not just be cheap. It had to feel authentic.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if a cannabis brand was built to reflect how heavy users actually see themselves, rather than how marketers imagine them?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Through ethnographic research, we found that heavy users carry hostility toward "big weed." This audience does not want to be educated or elevated. They want to be recognized.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built the brand around a persona called "The Hot Couch Guy", an honest, affectionate representation of heavy user's self-image. Not aspirational, not condescending.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We started with ethnographic research approach, spending time with Canadian heavy users to map their relationship with cannabis, media consumption, brand perceptions, and purchase drivers. We developed consumer positioning centred on "The Hot Couch Guy" persona. The brand identity drew from dark, atmospheric visuals, misty forests, moody lighting, a halo-over-mountain logo. We used collectible-style strain cards featuring strain information including terpene profiles, flavour notes, and descriptions in audience's own language. We developed brand architecture, paid and organic social asset development, and sales assets including merch.

proof: Holy Mountain launched in December 2022 and received strong consumer reviews
Brand's visual identity and strain card system generated organic social sharing
Positioning gave Organigram credible entry into value segment built on audience truth rather than price alone
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Tyler Owens, Organigram, Director, Brand & Trade
Mark McKay, Organigram, Director of Communications and Digital Strategy

title: SHRED: BRAND ARCHITECTURE & SOCIAL
summary: Made radical simplicity the entire brand identity in a heavily regulated category

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: SHRED launched as the first pre-milled cannabis product on the Canadian market. The brand had product differentiation but no strategic identity. We positioned SHRED around radical simplicity: in product, messaging, and visual identity. Cannabis consumers who buy pre-milled flower want something that works, every time, without fuss. The brand voice matched: direct, funny, zero pretension. SHRED remains one of the top-performing cannabis brands in Canada.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: SHRED launched as the first pre-milled cannabis product on the Canadian market, a format innovation offering genuine convenience. But the brand had no strategic identity.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Build a brand architecture that would differentiate SHRED from the THC-and-terpene arms race, then bring it to life through social content and digital campaigns.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Canadian cannabis marketing operates under strict regulatory constraints. Most brands default to product shots and potency stats. SHRED needed a brand voice that cut through sameness without breaking compliance. The product's core benefit risked reading as low-effort.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if a cannabis brand's personality was as uncomplicated as its product? What if simplicity was not a limitation but the entire identity?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Cannabis consumers who buy pre-milled flower are not looking for a connoisseur experience. They want something that works, every time, without fuss.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We positioned SHRED around radical simplicity, in product, in messaging, in visual identity. The communications framework celebrated convenience as a feature.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We developed the brand architecture, defining SHRED's personality, voice, visual identity, and messaging hierarchy. We built communications strategy and content planning framework, then executed paid and organic social asset development, sales tools, and digital/programmatic display campaigns. For social, we implemented a reactive content strategy that used topical moments and cultural trends. We designed and built the full brand website.

proof: 200% increase in followers
776% increase in organic impressions
Top-3 share of voice among cannabis brands online
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Tyler Owens, Organigram, Director, Brand & Trade
Mark McKay, Organigram, Director of Communications and Digital Strategy

title: SOLESAVY: STATE OF SNEAKERS
summary: Turned audience research into the brand's primary top-of-funnel content asset

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: SoleSavy, a membership community for sneaker collectors, had a loyal early base but needed to scale. We designed research to serve dual purposes: audience intelligence and external content marketing. We fielded an ethnographic study combining surveys with interviews and behavioural analysis, then synthesized findings into "The State of Sneakers, Vol. 1" — a designed white paper with data visualizations and persona profiles. Every finding laddered up to the membership value proposition. The white paper became SoleSavy's primary top-of-funnel content asset. Audience segmentation informed product development and marketing targeting.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: SoleSavy launched as a membership community for sneaker collectors frustrated by bots, resellers, and increasingly difficult odds of limited-edition drops. The company had a loyal early base but needed to scale.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Understand the current state of sneaker culture well enough to produce a credible white paper and content series that would position SoleSavy as category authority and drive new memberships.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Sneaker culture is tribal, fast-moving, and deeply skeptical of corporate messaging. A brand research piece could easily read as patronizing if the methodology was not rigorous or findings were not genuinely useful.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the research itself was the product, a document the community would share, cite, and argue about, because it reflected their world back to them with clarity they had not seen?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: The typical sneaker enthusiast profile did not match the caricature. Our research showed a 33-year-old, well-educated professional with disposable income, living in a metropolitan area. Forty percent got into sneakers in the previous five years. The community was feeling increasingly locked out of drops that defined the culture.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We designed the research to serve dual purposes: internal audience intelligence and external content marketing demonstrating expertise. Every finding was structured to ladder up to membership value proposition.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We designed and fielded an extensive ethnographic study combining quantitative surveys with qualitative interviews and behavioural analysis. The survey mapped demographics, spending behaviour, collection size, and entry points into sneaker culture. We synthesized findings into "The State of Sneakers, Vol. 1," a designed white paper with data visualizations and persona profiles. We produced a social content series pulling individual data points into shareable assets optimized for Instagram and Twitter.

proof: White paper became SoleSavy's primary top-of-funnel content asset
Social assets drove engagement and sharing within sneaker community
Audience segmentation informed internal product development and marketing targeting
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Research Lead

title: WYLD: CANADIAN MARKET ENTRY
summary: Fastest-growing edible brand in Canada with compostable packaging innovation

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Wyld was the top-selling cannabis gummy in the U.S. but had no Canadian presence. We positioned the entry around product authenticity, innovation (Canada's first compostable edibles packaging, which we designed and procured), and values alignment. We navigated province-specific compliance to secure listings for all 10 SKUs in five provinces.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Wyld had become the top-selling cannabis gummy brand in the United States. But Canadian brand awareness and affinity were non-existent. The Canadian edibles market had its own competitive dynamics.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Adapt Wyld's brand for Canadian consumers, ensure regulatory compliance across multiple provinces, and execute a market entry strategy that would achieve immediate distribution and sales velocity.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The Canadian cannabis edibles market was already crowded with local and regional brands. Wyld risked being dismissed as a foreign import. Provincial compliance requirements varied significantly.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if Wyld's U.S. success was reframed not as a foreign advantage but as proof of product quality, and the Canadian entry became an opportunity to lead on sustainability and innovation?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Competitive analysis revealed that less-localized competitors had gaps: inconsistent sustainability claims, generic flavour profiles, weak community engagement. Wyld's genuine product differentiators gave us concrete proof points.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We positioned the Canadian entry around three themes: product authenticity (real fruit, unique flavours), innovation (first compostable edibles packaging in Canada), and values alignment (transparency, quality, sustainability).

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We conducted edibles category research and competitive market mapping specific to the Canadian market. We developed all creative materials localized for the Canadian market. Packaging was a major workstream: we designed and procured Canada's first compostable edibles packaging. We produced trade marketing materials and in-store display designs. We wrote social media compliance guide addressing specific regulatory constraints. We developed digital content strategy and retail strategy adaptation. We executed market entry across provincial government boards.

proof: Fastest-growing edible brand in Canada in 2022 (+16.5% compound monthly growth rate)
Winner of every "Edible of the Year" award in Canada in launch year
ADCANN "Packaging of the Year" for compostable packaging innovation
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Ben Gaines, Director of Marketing, Wyld Cannabis
Cannabis Act compliance (Canada); Provincial regulatory requirements

title: CRUUZY: IT'S ALL CRUUZY
summary: Built personality-driven cannabis brand acquired within nine months of launch

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Galaxie needed a new recreational cannabis brand from scratch. Machine-learning audience segmentation revealed "the emerging enthusiast": daily users who increased consumption post-legalization and wanted a brand matching their energy. We followed the White Claw model — build the lifestyle brand first, let the product live inside it. Cruuzy was visually exciting with effortless energy but deliberately ambiguous in cultural references. The entire build took four months. First limited-time flower sold out in three weeks. Brand acquired by The Green Organic Dutchman within nine months of launch.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Galaxie, a licensed producer, needed a new recreational cannabis brand but had no consumer strategy, identity, or go-to-market plan. Sister Merci was given full creative and strategic ownership.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create a complete cannabis brand, from name through package design through launch, that could compete for the daily-use consumer in a rapidly commoditizing market.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: The Canadian cannabis market was flooded with brands that looked and sounded the same. The value segment was a race to the bottom on price. Premium brands leaned on craft narratives consumers could not verify.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if a cannabis brand felt less like a cannabis brand and more like a lifestyle product you would find at a surf shop or record store?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Machine-learning-driven audience segmentation revealed "the emerging enthusiast": daily users who started or increased consumption post-legalization. They were building a new consumption habit and wanted a brand matching their energy.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We followed the White Claw model: build the lifestyle brand first, let the product live inside it. Cruuzy was designed to be visually exciting with effortless energy but deliberately ambiguous in cultural references.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Full-scope brand build. We ran audience research and segmentation using machine-learning clustering against cannabis retail behaviour data. We developed the brand name, logo, and visual identity. We designed all packaging (three vape SKUs: Sugar Spice Indica, Citrus Ice Sativa, Mango Twist Hybrid) with illustration-forward design and retro-surf aesthetic. We produced merchandise displayers for retail, sales collateral for provincial buyers, content planning and social assets, and influencer engagement program. The entire process took four months.

proof: Cruuzy's first limited-time-offer flower sold out in three weeks after launch
Brand was on market for nine months before being acquired by The Green Organic Dutchman
Speed of acquisition validated both brand strategy and market positioning
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Erica Emery, Galaxie Brands, Director, Product & Strategic Development
Jill Garcia, Galaxie Brands, Brand Manager

title: OFFSIDE CANNABIS: SHOP AROUND
summary: Built retail brand that honours actual cannabis culture inside the legal framework

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Legal cannabis retail in Canada splits between sterile pharmacy and generic lifestyle. Neither honours actual cannabis culture. The target consumer identifies with cannabis — legalization was welcome, but it came with a sanitization of the culture they loved. We built OFFSIDE to celebrate being outside the mainstream. The name references breaking rules. The visual identity, retail experience, and brand voice were designed to feel like a counterculture space operating inside the legal framework.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: A value-oriented cannabis retailer was operating under the name "Spyder Cannabis." The company wanted to rebrand around the legacy cannabis consumer.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Name and create the complete brand, identity, assets, and retail experience, for a cannabis retailer targeting consumers who cherish the countercultural heritage of cannabis.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Legal cannabis retail tends to split between sterile pharmacy and generic lifestyle. Neither honours actual cannabis culture. The brand needed to reference that history without feeling nostalgic or exclusionary.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if a legal cannabis store could feel like the places where cannabis culture actually lived, not a dispensary trying to look like an Apple Store?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: The target consumer does not just buy cannabis. They identify with it. A retail brand that acknowledged the tension between legalization and culture would earn loyalty that price alone could not.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built OFFSIDE as a brand that celebrates being outside the mainstream. The name references the act of breaking rules. The visual identity, retail experience, and brand voice were designed to feel like a counterculture space operating inside the legal framework.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Full-scope brand build: audience segmentation and targeting, brand naming (OFFSIDE), brand development and architecture, logo and visual identity design (distinctive purple palette, gradient circle mark, clean modern typography), retail space design, package design, content strategy, social media engagement approach, and production of all marketing collateral.

proof: OFFSIDE launched with a complete brand system spanning retail, digital, collateral, and packaging
Retailer achieved distinct market position in crowded cannabis retail market
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead

title: SIMPLY BARE: NOTHING ADDED
summary: Extended a growing philosophy into a premium brand identity: nothing added, nothing taken away

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Simply Bare is an organic craft cannabis brand grown on BC's Sunshine Coast. Premium branding defaults to either dark luxury or rustic craft — both overcrowded. The brand's origin was not just geography: the light, the stone, the coast, and the stripped-back approach to growing were the brand. We built the visual identity as an extension of the growing philosophy — nothing added, nothing taken away — which carried over to the brand strategy, becoming the centrepiece of the brand tone of voice and personality. We art-directed a photoshoot with Maya Visnyei, created strain cards and educational materials, and designed packaging with a terracotta-toned palette and an embossed clay brand mark.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Simply Bare was an organic craft cannabis brand grown on BC's Sunshine Coast with clear product philosophy: minimal intervention, maximum quality. The brand had credentials to compete at premium end but lacked visual identity and brand system.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Develop a brand architecture and visual identity that would establish Simply Bare as the benchmark for organic craft cannabis in North America.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Premium cannabis branding tends to default to two overcrowded modes: dark and moody luxury or rustic craft. Simply Bare needed visual language that read as premium and craft without cliche.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the brand's visual identity followed the same principle as its growing philosophy, strip away everything that does not belong, and let what remains speak?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: The brand's origin on BC's Sunshine Coast was the entire aesthetic and philosophical foundation. The light, the stone, the coast, the stripped-back approach.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built the visual identity as an extension of the growing philosophy: nothing added, nothing taken away. Every visual decision was governed by restraint.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We developed the full brand architecture. For visual identity, we art directed and produced a brand photoshoot with award-winning photographer Maya Visnyei, creating hero assets that placed product against natural textures in soft, natural light. We designed and produced strain cards, grower's notes, and an education booklet. We built the brand website and developed a social content design system. The terracotta-toned packaging and embossed clay brand mark became recognizable shelf signals.

proof: Simply Bare launched with a visual identity that immediately distinguished it on shelf and in digital channels
Brand photography, strain cards, and education materials became core sales tools for budtender education
Brand established itself as a premium organic reference point in the Canadian market
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead
Andy von Hirschberg, Brand Manager, Rubicon Organics

title: HERBERT LABS: BRAND STRATEGY
summary: Pivoted to influencer-led soft launch when pandemic disrupted traditional rollout

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Herbert Labs brought Greenhouse Juices' plant-based wellness commitment into cannabis beverages. Functional cannabis beverages were new to Canada. We built positioning around three pillars: audience ("for people who want to grow"), functional framing ("trust your gut"), and product truth ("plant power in a bottle"). When COVID-19 arrived weeks before launch, we pivoted to an influencer program identifying 25 health and wellness influencers aligned with Herbert's positioning.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Herbert Labs was born from Greenhouse Juices, bringing commitment to plant-based wellness into the cannabis beverage space. The brand existed only as a product line with no market-facing identity.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Create brand strategy, consumer segmentation, positioning, and go-to-market plan that would launch Herbert into a nascent and crowded functional beverage market.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Functional cannabis beverages were new. Consumers did not have a frame of reference. The brand needed to educate without lecturing, differentiate on function without making health claims, and establish trust where most purchase decisions were driven by THC or CBD content. Then COVID-19 arrived weeks before planned launch.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the positioning did not lead with cannabis at all, but with the aspiration that cannabis-curious wellness consumers already held?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Through consumer segmentation work, we identified that the target audience defined themselves not by what they consumed but by how they wanted to live. Cannabis was a tool, not an identity.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We built the positioning around three pillars: audience ("for people who want to grow"), functional framing ("trust your gut"), and product truth ("plant power in a bottle"). The go-to-market plan was designed for flexibility.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: We conducted consumer segmentation study to identify and profile target audience. We developed the complete brand architecture: purpose, personality, positioning, voice, visual identity guidelines, and messaging hierarchy. For go-to-market, we developed an influencer program identifying 25 health and wellness influencers. We conducted 40 interviews and shop-alongs with target consumers and ran 3 listening sessions with brand ambassadors.

proof: Despite zero marketing spend, the influencer-led soft launch produced 200+ pieces of earned digital content
25 influencers posted multiple stories about the brand and products
The Message (Campaign magazine) covered the pivot as case study in launching during economic shutdown
BevNET reviewed the product line
Brand architecture became foundation for all subsequent Herbert marketing
team: PK Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Strategy Lead

title: AIR CANADA: AEROPLAN BREAKUP
summary: Turned loyalty program breakup into $2+ billion value creation play

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Air Canada was ending its contract with Aimia Inc. and building its own loyalty program. The challenge: telling 5 million Aeroplan members without causing a trust collapse or panic redemptions. Two weeks before the scheduled announcement, Aimia leaked to media, compressing the rollout into 24 hours. Consumer research and digital ethnography had shown us that Canadians' relationship with loyalty points is fundamentally emotional. We designed an offensive communications strategy built to pre-empt that emotional response: a pre-dawn briefing with Canada's most influential travel rewards voices, a real-time Q&A microsite, and messaging calibrated to frame the split as customer-positive.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Aeroplan was born in 1984 as Air Canada's in-house frequent flyer program. After financial fallout from 9/11 and SARS, Air Canada spun it off and sold it. By 2016, Air Canada had decided not to renew the contract with Aimia Inc.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Tell 5 million Aeroplan members that Air Canada was walking away from Aimia without causing a trust collapse, negative media cycle, or stampede of panic redemptions.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Canada is one of the most loyalty-obsessed consumer markets. Over 95% of Canadians belong to at least one program. Aeroplan members treated their miles as real savings, accumulated over years. The team had a cautionary tale: Air Miles announced it would expire unused miles and was forced into a humiliating public reversal.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the announcement could be designed to avoid panic and to actually deepen customer trust in Air Canada by demonstrating the airline cared more about its customers' points than the company managing them?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: Digital ethnography and consumer interviews revealed that Canadians' relationship with loyalty points is fundamentally emotional. The first response to any threat is a visceral fear that accumulated savings are at risk.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We designed an offensive communications program. The operating assumption was that we had a short window to own the narrative. The strategy was built to pre-empt the emotional response.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Consumer research and digital ethnography combined with in-depth consumer interviews to understand how Aeroplan members would respond. Points influencer pre-dawn briefing: before the press release went live, we led a briefing with Canada's most influential travel rewards voices. Real-time Q&A microsite: we developed a dedicated customer-facing hub at aircanada.com/loyalty. The site was still being built when Aimia's leak forced the launch; the team completed and published it within 24 hours. Messaging architecture: all official messaging was calibrated to frame the split as customer-positive.

proof: Stock price lifted 10.5% on announcement
Media coverage focused on business logic and reassurance, not panic
Aeroplan reacquired for $450M in 2018; relaunched in 2020
Program grew from 5M to 9M+ active members
Won Program of the Year, Best Elite Program, Best Promotion at 2025 Freddie Awards
team: Paul (PK) Lawton, Digital Strategist | Research lead, communications strategy, influencer briefing program, Q&A microsite concept and content
Madeline Long-Duke, VP, Corporate, Weber Shandwick
Amanda Wood, Creative Director, Weber Shandwick
Air Canada: Andy Shibata, VP Brand, Air Canada; Derek Whitworth, Customer Care, Air Canada; Mark Nasr, EVP & COO, Air Canada
Public filings, press releases, and published reporting; no proprietary internal communications or unreleased financial projections

title: NIA HEALTH: MARKET ENTRY
summary: Used single flagship research report to generate 66M earned impressions and establish market credibility

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Nia Health had technology and traction but no public narrative. We designed a single-asset content strategy: one flagship research report substantial enough to function as a credibility instrument on its own. We ran a research audit across peer-reviewed literature, layered external research over Nia's anonymized diagnostics, and produced a 10,000-word, 35-page report. Every other asset — messaging, talking points, social content, media pitches, investor materials — was derived from that single source.

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: Canada's healthcare system was under sustained pressure. Health-tech platforms were emerging but entering the market with consumer-facing messaging as wellness brands. Nia Health had technology and traction but no public narrative.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.text: Give Nia Health a market-entry communications strategy built on defensible positioning, a flagship piece of intellectual property that could anchor sustained media activity, and executive messaging infrastructure.

sections.heading: Challenge
sections.text: Health-tech companies entering Canada face credibility problems from two directions. The medical establishment is skeptical. And consumers have developed thick filters against health claims that sound like advertising.

sections.heading: Opportunity
sections.text: What if the same data that proved the platform worked could tell the story of why the platform was necessary? What if that story, told rigorously enough, could bypass traditional media gatekeeping?

sections.heading: Insight
sections.text: The gap Nia Health was filling was not a market gap. It was a system gap. Canadians were not choosing health-tech because they preferred it to doctors; they were turning to it because the system could not see them fast enough.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.text: We designed a single-asset content strategy. Rather than launching with scattered assets, we built the entire market entry around one flagship research report. Every other asset would be derived from that single source.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.text: Embedded research partnership: we operated as an embedded extension of Nia Health team. External research audit: we led a research audit across peer-reviewed literature in health sciences, sociology, and anthropology. Data synthesis: we layered external research over Nia's internal anonymized member diagnostics. Strategic positioning: from the research synthesis, we repositioned Nia Health as a necessary complement to an at-capacity system. Flagship report production: a 10,000-word, 35-page research report synthesizing diagnostic and clinical outcomes data. Messaging framework and executive positioning: we built talking points for CEO Sameer Dhar. Sustained content harvest: we designed the report to be modular, insights, charts, data points could be extracted and reformatted as standalone social assets.

proof: 106 earned media hits including BNN Bloomberg, CBC, National Post
66M earned impressions, continuing to accumulate
CEO went from unknown founder to recognized voice in health-tech
Report served as investor validation during fundraising
team: Paul (PK) Lawton, Chief Strategy Officer, Sister Merci | Research lead, strategic positioning, whitepaper content strategy, messaging framework

title: SHOPPERS DRUG MART: PROJECT HOUSTON
summary: Designed go-to-market strategy to reposition pharmacy as health services platform

sections.heading: Summary
sections.text: Canadian provinces were expanding pharmacy scope: prescribing for minor ailments, administering vaccinations, offering clinical services. SDM saw an opportunity to transform pharmacy from dispensing into a health services platform, but the consumer relationship was transactional. Consumer research revealed that SDM's broad "wellness" promise was a strategic trap — every category was already promising wellness. We designed a pharmacist-first strategy: all communications ladder up to a single idea before individual services launch. We replaced trigger-based consumer journeys with an occasion-state model built around five distinct moments.

sections.heading: Background
